    44→
    45→***
    46→
    47→# Editing this README
    48→
    49→When you're ready to make this README your own, just edit this file and use the handy template below (or feel free to structure it however you want - this is just a starting point!). Thanks to [makeareadme.com](https://www.makeareadme.com/) for this template.
    50→
    51→## Suggestions for a good README
    52→
    53→Every project is different, so consider which of these sections apply to yours. The sections used in the template are suggestions for most open source projects. Also keep in mind that while a README can be too long and detailed, too long is better than too short. If you think your README is too long, consider utilizing another form of documentation rather than cutting out information.
    54→
    55→## Name
    56→Choose a self-explaining name for your project.
    57→
    58→## Description
    59→Let people know what your project can do specifically. Provide context and add a link to any reference visitors might be unfamiliar with. A list of Features or a Background subsection can also be added here. If there are alternatives to your project, this is a good place to list differentiating factors.
    60→
    61→## Badges
    62→On some READMEs, you may see small images that convey metadata, such as whether or not all the tests are passing for the project. You can use Shields to add some to your README. Many services also have instructions for adding a badge.
    63→
    64→## Visuals
    65→Depending on what you are making, it can be a good idea to include screenshots or even a video (you'll frequently see GIFs rather than actual videos). Tools like ttygif can help, but check out Asciinema for a more sophisticated method.
    66→
    67→## Installation
    68→Within a particular ecosystem, there may be a common way of installing things, such as using Yarn, NuGet, or Homebrew. However, consider the possibility that whoever is reading your README is a novice and would like more guidance. Listing specific steps helps remove ambiguity and gets people to using your project as quickly as possible. If it only runs in a specific context like a particular programming language version or operating system or has dependencies that have to be installed manually, also add a Requirements subsection.
    69→
    70→## Usage
    71→Use examples liberally, and show the expected output if you can. It's helpful to have inline the smallest example of usage that you can demonstrate, while providing links to more sophisticated examples if they are too long to reasonably include in the README.
    72→
    73→## Support
    74→Tell people where they can go to for help. It can be any combination of an issue tracker, a chat room, an email address, etc.
    75→
    76→## Roadmap
    77→If you have ideas for releases in the future, it is a good idea to list them in the README.
    78→
    79→## Contributing
    80→State if you are open to contributions and what your requirements are for accepting them.
    81→
    82→For people who want to make changes to your project, it's helpful to have some documentation on how to get started. Perhaps there is a script that they should run or some environment variables that they need to set. Make these steps explicit. These instructions could also be useful to your future self.
    83→
    84→You can also document commands to lint the code or run tests. These steps help to ensure high code quality and reduce the likelihood that the changes inadvertently break something. Having instructions for running tests is especially helpful if it requires external setup, such as starting a Selenium server for testing in a browser.
    85→
    86→## Authors and acknowledgment
    87→Show your appreciation to those who have contributed to the project.
    88→
    89→## License
    90→For open source projects, say how it is licensed.
    91→
    92→## Project status
    93→If you have run out of energy or time for your project, put a note at the top of the README saying that development has slowed down or stopped completely. Someone may choose to fork your project or volunteer to step in as a maintainer or owner, allowing your project to keep going. You can also make an explicit request for maintainers.
    94→

1→# Organizational Documentation Repository
     2→
     3→[![Validate Documentation](https://github.com/SafariCircuitsLLC/organizational-docs/actions/workflows/validate-docs.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/SafariCircuitsLLC/organizational-docs/actions/workflows/validate-docs.yml)
     4→
     5→A domain-driven documentation architecture for Safari Circuits, designed for AI agent integration and cross-functional knowledge management.
     6→
     7→## Repository Purpose
     8→
     9→This repository serves as the central knowledge base for:
    10→- **Business Domain Knowledge** - Documented processes, entities, and integrations for each organizational domain
    11→- **Product Documentation** - Technical documentation for Safari Trace and its modules
    12→- **AI Agent Resources** - Skills, prompts, and context bundles for Claude and other AI assistants
    13→- **Project Management** - Active projects, architecture decisions, and milestones
    14→- **Operational Runbooks** - Deployment, incident response, and maintenance procedures
    15→
    16→## Repository Structure
    17→
    18→```
    19→organizational-docs/
    20→├── .claude/                    # AI Agent Configuration
    21→├── _meta/                      # Documentation metadata & indexes
    22→├── domains/                    # Business Domain Knowledge (DDD Bounded Contexts)
    23→│   ├── company-info/
    24→│   ├── engineering/
    25→│   ├── finance/
    26→│   ├── human-resources/
    27→│   ├── information-technology/
    28→│   ├── manufacturing/
    29→│   ├── quality/
    30→│   ├── sales-marketing/
    31→│   └── supply-chain/
    32→├── products/                   # Product/Module Documentation
    33→│   ├── safari-trace/
    34→│   ├── safari-studio/
    35→│   ├── safari-assist/
    36→│   └── safari-analyze/
    37→├── agents/                     # AI Agent Resources
    38→│   ├── skills/
    39→│   ├── prompts/
    40→│   └── contexts/
    41→├── shared/                     # Cross-Cutting Concerns
    42→│   ├── api-standards/
    43→│   ├── branding/
    44→│   ├── security/
    45→│   └── integration-patterns/
    46→├── projects/                   # Project Management
    47→│   ├── active/
    48→│   ├── templates/
    49→│   └── archive/
    50→└── runbooks/                   # Operational Procedures
    51→    ├── deployment/
    52→    ├── incidents/
    53→    └── maintenance/
    54→```
    55→
    56→## Documentation Index
    57→
    58→See [`index.yaml`](index.yaml) for a machine-readable catalog of all documentation, including:
    59→- **Standards** (STD-XXX) - Documentation architecture and conventions
    60→- **Work Instructions** (WI-XXX) - Step-by-step workflows
    61→- **Skills** (SKILL-XXX) - AI agent capabilities
    62→- All runbooks, domains, products, and projects
    63→
    64→## Quick Navigation
    65→
    66→### Business Domains
    67→
    68→| Domain | Description | Key Systems |
    69→|--------|-------------|-------------|
    70→| [Company Info](domains/company-info/) | Calendar, org chart, policies | SharePoint, Intranet |
    71→| [Engineering](domains/engineering/) | CAD, ECOs, BOM management | CAD Systems, PLM |
    72→| [Finance](domains/finance/) | Accounting, budgeting, reporting | Planful |
    73→| [Human Resources](domains/human-resources/) | Payroll, benefits, onboarding | HRIS, Payroll System |
    74→| [Information Technology](domains/information-technology/) | IT systems, infrastructure | AD, Monitoring Stack |
    75→| [Manufacturing](domains/manufacturing/) | Production, work orders, shop floor | MES, ERP |
    76→| [Quality](domains/quality/) | QC, inspections, certifications | QMS |
    77→| [Sales & Marketing](domains/sales-marketing/) | CRM, quoting, customer portal | CRM System |
    78→| [Supply Chain](domains/supply-chain/) | Purchasing, inventory, suppliers | ERP, Supplier Portal |
    79→
    80→### Safari Trace Modules
    81→
    82→| Module | Status | Description |
    83→|--------|--------|-------------|
    84→| [Safari Studio](products/safari-studio/) | Available | AI-powered diagram and artifact generation |
    85→| [Safari Assist](products/safari-assist/) | Coming Soon | AI assistant with knowledge base search |
    86→| [Safari Analyze](products/safari-analyze/) | Coming Soon | Data visualization and BI dashboards |
    87→
    88→## For AI Agents
    89→
    90→This repository is structured for optimal AI agent consumption:
    91→
    92→1. **Skills** (`agents/skills/`) - Reusable task definitions agents can execute
    93→2. **Prompts** (`agents/prompts/`) - Pre-built system and task prompts
    94→3. **Contexts** (`agents/contexts/`) - Domain-specific context bundles
    95→4. **Domain Knowledge** (`domains/`) - Business process and entity documentation
    96→
    97→### Claude Code Integration
    98→
    99→Add this repository to your Claude Code context:
   100→```bash
   101→cd /path/to/your/project
   102→# Reference documentation in CLAUDE.md or project context
   103→```

   108→

content.text: Let me now check the organizational-docs to understand the unified documentation approach:

content.content:
1→# Contributing to Organizational Documentation
     2→
     3→This guide covers how to contribute to and maintain documentation in this repository.
     4→
     5→## Documentation Principles
     6→
     7→1. **AI-First** - Write documentation that AI agents can parse and act upon
     8→2. **Domain-Driven** - Keep documentation within its appropriate bounded context
     9→3. **Living Documentation** - Update docs as systems and processes change
    10→4. **Single Source of Truth** - Avoid duplicating information across files
    11→
    12→## File Naming Conventions
    13→
    14→### General Rules
    15→- Use lowercase with hyphens: `engineering-change-order.md`
    16→- Use descriptive names: `budget-approval-workflow.md` not `workflow1.md`
    17→- README.md files provide directory overviews
    18→
    19→### Formal Document Types
    20→
    21→Documents with an ID prefix follow this naming pattern:
    22→
    23→```
    24→{TYPE}-{NUMBER}-{kebab-case-title}.md
    25→```
    26→
    27→| Type | Prefix | Description | Location |
    28→|------|--------|-------------|----------|
    29→| Policy | POL | WHY - principles, constraints | `/policies/` |
    30→| Standard | STD | WHAT - requirements, specifications | `/_meta/` or `/shared/` |
    31→| Procedure | SOP | FLOW - process sequences | `/procedures/` |
    32→| Work Instruction | WI | HOW - step-by-step task execution | `/runbooks/{category}/` |
    33→| Skill | SKILL | AI agent capabilities | `/agents/skills/{category}/` |
    34→
    35→**Examples:**
    36→- `STD-001-documentation-architecture.md`
    37→- `WI-001-claude-tool-selection.md`
    38→- `SKILL-001-documentation-commit.md`
    39→
    40→### Required Metadata Header
    41→
    42→All formal documents must include a YAML metadata block:
    43→
    44→```yaml
    45→id: [TYPE]-[NUMBER]        # Unique identifier (e.g., WI-001)
    46→title: string              # Human-readable name
    47→domain: string             # Business domain (governance, productivity, etc.)
    48→tags: [array]              # For search/retrieval
    49→visualization: string      # Suggested render type (flowchart, decision-tree, etc.)
    50→estimated_time: string     # Reading/execution time (optional)
    51→last_updated: date         # ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD)
    52→```
    53→
    54→See [STD-001: Documentation Architecture](_meta/documentation-architecture.md) for complete standards.
    55→
    56→## Document Structure
    57→
    58→### Domain Entity Documents
    59→
    60→```markdown
    61→# Entity Name
    62→
    63→## Overview
    64→Brief description of what this entity represents.
    65→
    66→## Attributes
    67→| Field | Type | Description |
    68→|-------|------|-------------|
    69→| id | string | Unique identifier |
    70→
    71→## Relationships
    72→- Related to: [Other Entity](../other-entity.md)
    73→
    74→## Business Rules
    75→- Rule 1: Description
    76→- Rule 2: Description
    77→
    78→## API Reference
    79→If applicable, link to API documentation.
    80→```
    81→
    82→### Process Documents
    83→
    84→```markdown
    85→# Process Name
    86→
    87→## Overview
    88→What this process accomplishes.
    89→
    90→## Trigger
    91→What initiates this process.
    92→
    93→## Steps
    94→1. Step one
    95→2. Step two
    96→3. Step three
    97→
    98→## Roles
    99→| Role | Responsibility |
   100→|------|----------------|
   101→| Initiator | Starts the process |
   102→| Approver | Reviews and approves |
   103→
   104→## Systems Involved
   105→- System A
   106→- System B
   107→
   108→## Exceptions
   109→How to handle edge cases.
   110→```
   111→
   112→### Skill Documents
   113→
   114→```markdown
   115→# Skill Name
   116→
   117→## Purpose
   118→What this skill enables an AI agent to do.
   119→
   120→## Trigger Phrases
   121→- "Create a new..."
   122→- "Generate..."
   123→
   124→## Required Context
   125→- Domain knowledge needed
   126→- Permissions required
   127→
   128→## Steps
   129→1. Action one
   130→2. Action two
   131→
   132→## Output
   133→What the skill produces.
   134→
   135→## Example
   136→Example invocation and result.
   137→```
   138→
   139→## Adding New Content
   140→
   141→### New Domain Entity
   142→1. Create file in `domains/<domain>/entities/`
   143→2. Follow entity document structure
   144→3. Update domain README.md
   145→4. Add to glossary if new terminology
   146→
   147→### New Process
   148→1. Create file in `domains/<domain>/processes/`
   149→2. Follow process document structure
   150→3. Link related entities
   151→4. Update domain context-map.md if cross-domain
   152→
   153→### New Skill
   154→1. Create file in `agents/skills/<category>/`
   155→2. Follow skill document structure
   156→3. Test with Claude Code
   157→4. Update skills README.md
   158→
   159→## Development Setup
   160→
   161→### Install Git Hooks
   162→
   163→After cloning the repository, set up the pre-commit hook for local STD-001 validation:
   164→
   165→```bash
   166→# Unix/macOS/WSL
   167→./scripts/setup-hooks.sh
   168→
   169→# Or manually
   170→git config core.hooksPath .githooks
   171→```
   172→
   173→The pre-commit hook will:
   174→- Validate documentation files against STD-001 before each commit
   175→- Block commits that don't meet naming or metadata requirements
   176→- Skip validation if no documentation files are staged
   177→
   178→To bypass the hook temporarily: `git commit --no-verify`
   179→
   180→### Requirements
   181→
   182→- **Node.js** (v18+) - Required for validation script
   183→
   184→## Review Process
   185→
   186→1. Create a branch for your changes
   187→2. Make documentation updates
   188→3. Ensure links are valid
   189→4. Run `node scripts/validate-std-001.js` to verify compliance
   190→5. Submit pull request
   191→6. Request review from domain owner
   192→
   193→### Branch Protection
   194→
   195→The `main` branch is protected with the following requirements:
   196→
   197→| Requirement | Description |
   198→|-------------|-------------|
   199→| **STD-001 Compliance Check** | All formal documents must pass validation |
   200→| **Status checks must pass** | CI workflow must complete successfully |
   201→
   202→PRs that fail the compliance check cannot be merged. Common failures:
   203→
   204→| Error | Fix |
   205→|-------|-----|
   206→| File name does not follow convention | Rename to `{TYPE}-{NUMBER}-{title}.md` |
   207→| Missing YAML metadata block | Add `## Metadata` section with required fields |
   208→| Missing required fields | Add `id`, `title`, `domain`, `tags`, `last_updated` |
   209→
   210→Run validation locally before pushing:
   211→```bash
   212→node scripts/validate-std-001.js
   213→```
   214→
   215→## Maintenance
   216→
   217→- Review documentation quarterly
   218→- Archive outdated content to `projects/archive/`
   219→- Update context maps when integrations change
   220→- Keep glossary current
   221→

1→# Safari Circuits Team Standards - Boris Workflow
     2→
     3→This global CLAUDE.md establishes team-wide best practices based on Boris Cherny's Claude Code workflow.
     4→
     5→## Core Principles (Always Follow)
     6→
     7→1. **Automatic workflow** - Session management, context checkpoints, and learnings happen automatically. Users never invoke workflow commands - they just work.
     8→2. **Think first, read the codebase** - Always investigate relevant files BEFORE making changes. Never speculate about code you haven't opened.
     9→3. **Check in before major changes** - Present your plan and get verification before significant modifications.
    10→4. **Explain changes at a high level** - Provide concise explanations of what changes you made and why.
    11→5. **Keep it simple** - Make every change as minimal as possible. Each change should impact as little code as possible.
    12→6. **Maintain documentation** - Keep architecture documentation current. Update CLAUDE.md with learnings when mistakes are made.
    13→7. **Never speculate** - If you haven't read a file, don't make claims about it.
    14→8. **Verification loops** - Tests, typecheck, and lint after every change.
    15→9. **Self-improving** - Capture learnings automatically. Every correction feeds back into better future sessions.
    16→
    17→---
    18→
    19→## Iterative Objectives (Inferred from Commit Patterns)
    20→
    21→Context matures through a lifecycle. Every session should advance context along this pipeline:
    22→
    23→```
    24→Idea → Learning → Skill → Standard → Governance
    25→  ↑                                      |
    26→  └──────────── Feedback Loop ───────────┘
    27→```
    28→
    29→### Automatic Actions Per Session
    30→
    31→| Objective | Claude Action | Trigger |
    32→|-----------|---------------|---------|
    33→| **Consolidate** | Always write to `organizational-docs`, never OneDrive | Any file creation |
    34→| **Automate** | Run `/end-work` automatically when task complete | Task completion signal |
    35→| **Capture** | Propose CLAUDE.md learning when I make a mistake | Correction received |
    36→| **Formalize** | Suggest skill creation when pattern repeats 3+ times | Pattern detection |
    37→| **Visualize** | Offer to create diagram when explaining architecture | Complex explanation |
    38→| **Govern** | Track context usage and flag staleness | Session checkpoint |
    39→| **Stay on topic** | Capture tangential thoughts via `/reminder`, route appropriately | Off-topic input |
    40→
    41→### Context Maturation Stages
    42→
    43→| Stage | Description | Location | Promotion Trigger |
    44→|-------|-------------|----------|-------------------|
    45→| **Idea** | Raw thought, unvalidated | `learning/ideas/backlog.md` | Groomed with acceptance criteria |
    46→| **Learning** | Validated insight from session | `learning/best-practices/` | Used successfully 3+ times |
    47→| **Skill** | Reusable encoded expertise | `skills/{name}/SKILL.md` | Stable, documented, tested |
    48→| **Standard** | Team-wide convention | `docs/standards/STD-*.md` | Adopted across projects |
    49→| **Governance** | Measured, enforced policy | CLAUDE.md or registry | Automated enforcement |
    50→
    51→### Evolution Metrics (from Jan 18-20 commits)
    52→
    53→| Area | Changes | Focus |
    54→|------|---------|-------|
    55→| Skills | 51 | Building AI expertise |
    56→| Commands | 32 | Workflow automation |
    57→| Branding | 29 | Visual identity |
    58→| Learning | 25 | Knowledge capture |
    59→| Strategy | 20 | Leadership docs |
    60→
    61→**Key insight:** Skills evolve fastest, followed by workflow automation. Prioritize capturing expertise as skills.
    62→
    63→---
    64→
    65→## Key Repository Paths
    66→
    67→**Known repositories - Claude should NEVER search for these:**
    68→
    69→| Repo | Local Path | Remote |
    70→|------|------------|--------|
    71→| **organizational-docs** | `C:\Users\dkmcintyre\organizational-docs` | SafariCircuitsLLC/organizational-docs |
    72→
    73→### Documentation Write Rules (ENFORCED)
    74→
    75→**MUST write to:** `C:\Users\dkmcintyre\organizational-docs`
    76→
    77→**NEVER write to:** OneDrive locations (archive only for CSVs, PDFs, HTML exports)
    78→
    79→### Key Documentation Paths
    80→
    81→| Content Type | Path in organizational-docs |
    82→|--------------|----------------------------|
    83→| Architecture docs | `domains/information-technology/architecture/` |
    84→| Integration patterns | `shared/integration-patterns/` |
    85→| Product docs | `products/{product}/` |
    86→| Active projects | `projects/active/{project}/` |
    87→| Claude workflow | `shared/claude-workflow/` |
    88→| Standards | `docs/standards/` |
    89→| Shareable deliverables | `deliverables/{audience}/` |
    90→
    91→---
    92→
    93→## Session Workflow (AUTOMATIC)
    94→
    95→```
    96→[First message] → AUTO-START → WORK → AUTO-CHECKPOINT → AUTO-VALIDATE → [Session ends] → AUTO-CLOSE
    97→```
    98→
    99→| Phase | Trigger | Claude Does (silently) |
   100→|-------|---------|------------------------|
   101→| **Start** | First user message | Load `~/.claude/state/phase.md`, check MCPs, load context |
   102→| **Work** | User requests | Plan mode for complex tasks, incremental changes |
   103→| **Checkpoint** | ~50 turns, task complete, topic shift | Update phase.md silently |
   104→| **Validate** | Code changes made | Run tests/lint in background |
   105→| **End** | User says bye, explicit close | Full checkpoint, update work items |
   106→
   107→**Context Health:** Fresh (< 30 turns) → Warm (30-60) → Hot (> 60, checkpoint needed)
   108→
   109→**Full details:** See `/checkpoint`, `/start-work`, `/end-work` skills
   110→
   111→### Focus Management (Staying on Topic)
   112→
   113→When user shares off-topic thoughts, **auto-invoke `/reminder`**:
   114→- Acknowledge briefly → Categorize → Confirm: `✓ [category]: "[item]" → [destination]` → Return to work
   115→
   116→---
   117→
   118→## Natural Language → Slash Command Mappings
   119→
   120→| User Says | Execute |
   121→|-----------|---------|
   122→| "start work", "begin session", "let's get started" | `/start-work` |
   123→| "end work", "wrap up", "done for now" | `/end-work` |
   124→| "what's the status", "where are we" | `/status` |
   125→| "validate", "check everything" | `/validate-work` |
   126→| "commit and push", "push changes" | `/quick-commit` |
   127→| "review changes", "what changed" | `/review-changes` |
   128→| "brain dump", "get this out of my head" | `/brain-dump` |
   129→| "reminder:", "don't forget", "we should" | `/reminder` |
   130→| "groom the backlog", "refine backlog" | `/groom` |
   131→| "checkpoint", "save progress" | `/checkpoint` |
   132→| "sync to devops", "update devops" | `/sync-devops` |
